segments.inferno-25-g18: Per = 'through' (poetic preposition introducing a space traversed). Tutt’ is the elided form of tutti ('all'); i cerchi = 'the circles' (definite plural). De lo 'nferno is an archaic/poetic sequence for del/in the Inferno (de + lo = of the); scuri is postpositive adjective forming 'dark circles of Hell'. The whole phrase is a prepositional phrase modifying vidi.
segments.inferno-25-g19: Non negates the following clause. Quel che = 'that which / the one who' (introduces a relative clause referring back to 'spirit'). Cadde is passato remoto of cadere ('fell'). A Tebe indicates location ('at Thebes'); giù da’ muri = 'down from the walls' (giù = down; da’ = poetic contraction of dai/da + i). Word order places the location after the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-25-g20: El is an archaic/heavy-stressed subject pronoun (he/it). Si fuggì is reflexive fuggire in passato remoto ('he fled away'); the reflexive si emphasizes the action upon the subject. Che introduces a result/clause of consequence: 'so that / and then'; non parlò più verbo = 'spoke no more a word' (parlò = passato remoto of parlare; più here means 'any longer').
segments.inferno-25-g21: E io vidi = 'and I saw' (passato remoto vidi). Un centauro is the direct object; pien di rabbia = 'full of rage' (pien is shortened form of pieno, adjective after the noun). Venir chiamando is a periphrasis: venir (infinitive) + gerund chiamando gives the sense 'come calling' — I saw him come while calling.
segments.inferno-25-g22: Ov’ is the elided form of ove (where); è is the present of essere ('is'). This is a vocative/exclamatory question (he is shouting), so present tense is natural in reported speech.
segments.inferno-25-g23: Repetition ov’ è + l’acerbo is 'where is the acerbo?' The article + adjective (l’acerbo) functions as a substantive: 'the harsh/bitter one' — an epithet used as a name. The sentence ends with a direct question in the centaur's cry.
segments.inferno-25-g24: A single punctuation mark concluding the previous line of dialogue; there are no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-25-g25: Maremma is an interjectory oath invoking the Maremma region ('Maremma!' used like an expletive). Non cred’ io = 'I do not believe' with inverted order (poetic elision of credo). Che introduces a subordinate clause; tante n’ abbia = 'that it has so many of them' where n’ = ne (of them) and abbia is present subjunctive of avere, used after a negative expression of belief.
segments.inferno-25-g26: Quante = 'as many as / how many' introducing a comparative amount. Bisce here means 'snakes' (the provided senses do not match the meaning, so it's treated as an irregular/archaic noun). Elli is an archaic pronoun used for 'he/it' (refers to the beast); avea is the imperfect of avere ('had'). Su per la groppa = 'up along his back' (su = up/on; groppa = animal's back). Infin (fino) ove comincia nostra labbia = 'as far as where our lip begins' (comincia present indicative 'begins'; nostra labbia = 'our lip/mouth').
segments.inferno-25-g47: A single semicolon (here rendered as an em dash in translation) marking a pause or break in the narrative; it connects what follows to the preceding clause.
segments.inferno-25-g48: "per che" is an archaic/conjunctive phrase meaning "wherefore/so that/therefore"; "nostra novella" = "our news/tale" (novella = report, tale) and "si ristette" is a reflexive/impersonal perfective verb (ristette, passato remoto) meaning "stopped/ceased" — literally "our tale stopped itself" or "our telling came to a halt."
segments.inferno-25-g49: "e" = "and"; "intendemmo" is the 1st person plural passato remoto of intendere and here means "we heard/listened to" (in older/poetic Italian intendere often = 'to hear'); "pur" = "still/still yet"; "ad essi" = "to them" (essi = those spirits); "poi" = "afterward/then." The whole phrase means "and afterward we still listened to them."
segments.inferno-25-g50: "Io non li conoscea" uses the imperfect (conoscea, older spelling of conosceva) meaning "I did not know/recognize them." "Li" is an unstressed object pronoun (here plural: 'them').
segments.inferno-25-g51: "ma" = "but"; "ei" is an archaic 3rd-person pronoun (here singular "he" rather than modern "egli"), the subject of "seguette"; "seguette" is passato remoto of seguire (or figuratively "continued/ensued/proceeded").
segments.inferno-25-g52: "come suol seguitar per alcun caso" is a parenthetical simile: "come" = "as"; "suol" = present tense 'is wont' (he/it is wont), "seguitar" (infinitive) = "to follow/ensue" in a proverbial expression; "per alcun caso" = "on some occasion/by some chance." Together: "as is wont to happen on some occasion."
segments.inferno-25-g53: "che" introduces the content clause; "l’un" = "one (of them)" (the apostrophe marks elision); "nomar" (older infinitive from 'nominare'/'nomare') = "to name/mention"; "un altro" = "another"; "convenette" is passato remoto of convenire used impersonally meaning "it was fitting/appropriate" (literally "it suited"). The clause: "that one should name another was fitting."
segments.inferno-25-g54: "dicendo" is the present participle/gerund of dire, here meaning "saying," introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-25-g55: Direct speech: the proper name "Cianfa" is called; "dove" = "where"; "fia" is the present subjunctive (archaic future-subjunctive use) of essere, here "may be/has been/is" in the sense "where is/has he ended up"; "rimaso" is a past participle meaning "remained/ended up." The phrase is an idiomatic question "Cianfa, where is he now?/where has he remained?"
segments.inferno-25-g56: Semicolon/pause marking a shift to the narrator's action; punctuation only.
segments.inferno-25-g57: "per ch'io" (per + che + io) begins a purpose clause: "so that I..."; "per" here is part of the conjunctional phrase and "ch'" = "che" = "that."
segments.inferno-25-g58: "acciò che" is a purposive/linking phrase meaning "so that" or "in order that." "'l duca" = "the duke/guide" (Virgil); "stesse" is imperfect subjunctive of stare (stare/stare attento) used after the purposive particle to express desired state "might be"; "attento" = "attentive." So the clause means "so that the duke might be attentive."
segments.inferno-25-g59: "mi puosi" is passato remoto of porre (old form puosi) with reflexive dative "mi" = "I put/placed for myself"; "'l dito" = "the finger" (l' = the article); "su" = "upon/on"; "dal mento al naso" = "from the chin to the nose." The whole: "I placed my finger on (my face), from chin to nose," a gesture to signal the duke to pay attention.
segments.inferno-25-g60: "Se tu se' or" = "If you are now" (se' = archaic contraction of sei/are); "or" = "now." This sets up a conditional address to the reader.
segments.inferno-25-g62: "a creder lento" = "to believe slowly" i.e. "slow to believe/slow in believing"; "ciò ch'io dirò" = "what I will say" (dirò = future of dire). The infinitive phrase expresses the reader's expected reaction to the forthcoming account.
segments.inferno-25-g63: "non sarà maraviglia" = "it will not be a wonder" — futuro semplice of essere (sarà) with negation, stating that surprise would be natural.
segments.inferno-25-g64: "ché" = "for/because"; "io che 'l vidi" = "I who saw it" (che introduces the relative clause; vidi = passato remoto of vedere).
segments.inferno-25-g65: "a pena" = idiomatically "hardly/with difficulty"; "il mi consento" = "I myself consent to it" where "il" is the neuter direct object pronoun "it" (referring to the thing seen) and "mi consento" = "I give my assent" (consento here in the 1st person present). The phrase means "I who saw it barely myself consent (to its truth)."
segments.inferno-25-g1: 'Al fine de le sue parole' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'at the end of his words': 'Al' is the contraction a + il ('at the'), 'fine' is a noun 'end', 'de' is an archaic form of di ('of'), and 'le sue parole' is feminine plural ('his/her words') with possessor following the article. The main clause follows: 'il ladro le mani alzò' inverts normal subject–verb order (subject 'il ladro' comes before verb here for emphasis), with 'alzò' the passato remoto (simple past) 'raised', and 'le mani' the direct object. 'con amendue le fiche' is an idiomatic phrase 'with both figs' (amendue = 'both', fiche = 'figs' — here an obscene hand-gesture).
segments.inferno-25-g2: 'gridando' is a present participle (gerund) meaning 'shouting' or 'crying out' and links to the main verb 'alzò' to indicate manner (how he raised his hands). It introduces the following direct quotation.
segments.inferno-25-g3: 'Togli' is the imperative singular of togliere ('remove, take away'), here an imperative address within the quotation.
segments.inferno-25-g4: Address in the vocative: 'Dio' = 'God'. It is the addressee of the imperative 'Togli'.
segments.inferno-25-g5: This clause contains an archaic contraction and inverted order: 'ch’ a te le squadro' = che a te le squadro. 'Ch’' = che ('that' or 'so that'); 'a te' = 'to/at you' (dative/vocative sense: directed toward you); 'le' is the feminine plural object 'them' (the figs); 'squadro' here is an action meaning 'I throw/aim/level/expose them at you' (an archaic use of squadrare/squadro). Grammatically, the verb form is 1st person singular present (I) within the quotation, completing the thief's imperative address.
segments.inferno-25-g6: Simple punctuation: end of the sentence (period).
segments.inferno-25-g7: 'Da indi in qua' is an idiomatic, slightly archaic locution meaning 'from then on' (literally 'from that place/time here'): 'Da' = 'from', 'indi' = 'thence/then' (archaic), 'in qua' = 'in this time/side'. 'mi fuor le serpi amiche' uses 'fuor' (archaic past form of furono/they were/appeared) with an indirect object 'mi' meaning 'to me' or 'upon me' — literally 'friendly snakes were to me' = 'friendly snakes came at me/appeared against me'. The subject 'le serpi amiche' follows the verb for poetic word order.
segments.inferno-25-g8: 'perch’ una li s’avvolse allora al collo' explains the cause: 'perch’' = perché ('because'/'for'), 'una' = 'one (of them)', 'li' here is an archaic dative/accusative pronoun referring back to the person attacked (roughly 'him' — the thief), 's’avvolse' is the pronominal verb avvolgersi in passato remoto/indicative (si avvolse = 'wrapped itself/itself wound'), 'allora' = 'then', 'al collo' = 'about the neck'. So: 'because one of them then coiled itself about his neck.'
segments.inferno-25-g9: 'come dicesse' = 'as if he were saying' (literally 'as he would say'), where 'dicesse' is the imperfect subjunctive used in reported or mimed speech to indicate manner. The quoted wording that follows ('Non vo’ che più diche’') is an archaic direct speech: 'Non vo’' = 'I do not want' (vo' = voglio), 'che più diche’' uses an archaic subjunctive form of dire ('diche’' ~ 'dica') and 'più' = 'any more', so the content is 'I do not want him to speak any more.'
segments.inferno-25-g10: 'e un’altra a le braccia' continues the action: 'e' = 'and', 'un’altra' = 'another (one)', 'a le braccia' = 'to/around the arms' (note that poetic spacing keeps 'a le' separate where modern Italian would contract to 'alle'). Grammatically, 'un’altra' is feminine because it refers back to 'serpe'.
segments.inferno-25-g11: 'e rilegollo' contains a verb with an enclitic pronoun: 'rilegò' (to bind again) + 'lo' (him/it). In context this functions as 'and it bound him again' — the serpent rewrapped/rebound the victim. The terse, poetic form drops auxiliary words and lets the enclitic attach directly.
segments.inferno-25-g12: 'ribadendo sé stessa sì dinanzi' uses the gerund 'ribadendo' (from ribadire, here 'fastening again' / 'driving in again'), followed by the reflexive emphasis 'sé stessa' ('itself'), and 'sì dinanzi' ('so in front' / 'thus before him'). Grammatically, the gerund gives a concurrent action describing how the serpent fixed itself in front of the victim.
segments.inferno-25-g13: 'che non potea con esse dare un crollo' is a result clause introduced by 'che' meaning 'so that' / 'so that it was the case that': 'non potea' is imperfect indicative of potere ('could not'), 'con esse' = 'with them' (the serpents), 'dare un crollo' literally 'to give/cause a shake/collapse' = 'to make even a single wrench/struggle'. Overall: 'so that he could not, with them, make a single shake.'
segments.inferno-25-g14: 'Ahi' is an interjection ('alas') addressing the city; 'Pistoia' is the vocative/name repeated for emphasis. The repetition is a rhetorical apostrophe to the city.
segments.inferno-25-g15: A single-word repetition of the city-name 'Pistoia' for rhetorical emphasis (again vocative).
segments.inferno-25-g16: 'ché non stanzi d’incenerarti sì che più non duri' is a complex exhortation: 'ché' here introduces a rhetorical question ('why'), 'non stanzi' uses an older form of 'stanziare/stanziarsi' meaning 'set about' / 'resolve' (here 'do you not set about'), 'd’incenerarti' = 'to incinerate/consume yourself' (infinitive with enclitic 'ti'), 'sì che' = 'so that', 'più non duri' = 'you no longer endure' where 'duri' is present subjunctive ('that you last/endure no longer'). Grammatically this is a why-question plus purpose/result clause.
segments.inferno-25-g17: 'poi che ’n mal fare il seme tuo avanzi?' is a causal clause 'since / seeing that': 'poi che' = 'since/now that', '’n mal fare' = 'in doing evil' (’n = in), 'il seme tuo' = 'your seed/offspring', and 'avanzi' is present indicative 'remains/persists' (here 'your seed continues on/in wrongdoing'). The verb 'avanzi' is used in the sense 'is left/continues' rather than its more literal sense 'surplus'.
segments.inferno-25-g66: Com’ is the contracted archaic form of come meaning “as.” io tenea uses the imperfect tense (tenea, 1st/3rd-person imperfect form in older spelling) to indicate a continuous past action (“I/ I was keeping”). levate is a past participle used adjectivally (“raised”) that depends on tenea and refers to le ciglia; in lor = ‘toward them’ (lor = them) shows direction/target of the gaze. Word order (verb before adjective before object) is typical of Dante’s poetic style.
segments.inferno-25-g67: e = “and.” un serpente = “a serpent.” con sei piè = “having/with six feet” (piè is an archaic form of piede). si lancia is a present (or vivid) reflexive usage of lanciare — “(it) launches/rushes itself” — used here for immediate vivid narration. dinanzi a l’uno = “in front of/at one (of them)” — a + l’uno indicates the target person.
segments.inferno-25-g68: e = “and.” tutto here functions adverbially, meaning “entirely/completely.” a lui = “to him” (indicates attachment to that one person). s’ is the reflexive clitic and appiglia is present indicative of appigliare meaning “fasten on / cling to / take hold of”; together s’appiglia = “clings to him.”
segments.inferno-25-g69: Co’ is a contracted form of con (‘with’). piè di mezzo = “middle feet” (the middle pair). li avvinse: avvinse is passato remoto (3rd person) of avvingere/avvincolare in the sense “seized/clasped,” and the clitic/li/article indicates the affected person; la pancia is the direct object, “the belly.” e con li anterïor le braccia prese = “and with the fore (anterïor) [feet] it took/seized the arms”; anterïor (with diaeresis) marks syllabification in the poem and prese is the past tense of prendere.
segments.inferno-25-g70: poi = “then.” li addentò: addentò is passato remoto of addentare, “to bite into” or “to sink one’s teeth into”; li is a clitic referring back to the persons/parts previously mentioned (so: “it bit them”). e l’una e l’altra guancia = “one cheek and the other,” a pair expression stressing that both cheeks (or both sides) were bitten.
segments.inferno-25-g71: li diretani = “the hind ones” (li + diretani) — diretani refers to the hind parts or hindquarters/haunches. a le cosce distese = “stretched toward the thighs” (a = toward; le cosce = the thighs; distese = past participle/adjective “outstretched/extended”), describing the position of the hind parts.
segments.inferno-25-g72: e miseli = miseli is mise + clitic (mise = passato remoto of mettere, “put/placed”; -li here marks the person affected), so “and he/it put/placed it.” la coda = “the tail.” tra ’mbedue = tra ambedue = “between the two.” e dietro per le ren sù la ritese = “and from behind, up along the loins, he drew it up”: dietro per le ren sù gives the path (“behind, along the loins, upward”) and ritese (past form) indicates the action of pulling/drawing up or stretching it up.
segments.inferno-25-g73: Ellera = “ivy.” abbarbicata is a past participle meaning “taken root” or “clung tightly” (abbarbicarsi = to take root/fasten oneself). mai non fue = a double-negative formula in older Italian meaning “never was” (fue = passato remoto of essere, “was”). ad alber sì = “to trees so” (sì = “so” — i.e., to that degree).
segments.inferno-25-g74: come = “as.” l’orribil fiera = “the horrible beast” (fiera = wild beast). per l’altrui membra = “by/through another’s limbs” (altrui = of others/another’s, showing possession; per indicates movement around or through). avviticchiò is passato remoto of avviticchiare, “twined/entangled/spiraled,” and le sue = “its own” (referring to the beast’s own limbs/tendrils) — so the beast twined its own about another’s limbs.
segments.inferno-25-g75: Poi is a simple temporal adverb meaning “then/afterwards” placed at the start. s’ is the clitic reflexive si (theyselves) contracted to s’ and combined with the verb; appiccar is the archaic/poetic infinitive-form used here in place of a past plural (appiccarono) to indicate a past action — together they mean “they stuck/attached themselves.”
segments.inferno-25-g76: come introduces a simile “as/like.” di expresses material/comparison (“of”). calda cera places the adjective before the noun in the expected poetic order: “hot wax.” fossero stati is the third-person plural pluperfect-subjunctive (congiuntivo trapassato) used in the simile to mean “as if they had been.”
segments.inferno-25-g77: e simply links the previous action to this one (“and”). mischiar is again the infinitival/archaic form used in place of a finite past (mischiarono) — here coordinating with the previous verb to mean “they mixed/mingled.” lor is the (poetic) enclitic form of loro used before a noun: “their.” colore is the direct object “color/appearance.”
segments.inferno-25-g78: né ... né is the correlative negative “neither ... nor.” l’ (elided il) + un/altro mark “the one/the other.” già here has a temporal sense (“by then/any longer”) and parea is the imperfect indicative of parere, “seemed.” quel ch’era is a relative clause “that which he was/that he had been,” with era as the imperfect of essere. The whole means neither looked any longer like what he had been.
segments.inferno-25-g79: come again introduces a comparative/simile “as.” procede is a present (3rd sg.) used here in the timeless descriptive simile “as (it) proceeds/comes forward.” innanzi is an adverb meaning “forward/in front.” da + l’ardore means “from the heat/ardour,” with da l’ (from + the) indicating origin.
segments.inferno-25-g80: per here means “up/along” (indicating motion along a thing). lo papiro is the definite noun “the papyrus” (object along which the color moves). suso is an adverb “up/above” placed after the noun in the inverted/poetic order: “up along the papyrus.”
segments.inferno-25-g81: un color bruno is a nominal phrase “a dark/brown color.” che introduces a relative clause. non è is present indicative “is not.” nero ancora = “black yet” with ancora meaning “yet/still” (here “not yet black”). e ’l bianco more : e links the clause; ’l (il) bianco “the white” is the contrasting part, and more is an older form meaning “dies/fades” (the white fades away).
segments.inferno-25-g125: Togliea is an archaic imperfect (3rd person singular) of togliere meaning ‘was taking away’ or ‘removed’; Dante places the verb first for poetic inversion. La coda fessa = ‘the split tail,’ where fessa (from fessare) is an adjective ‘split/cleft’ modifying coda. La figura che si perdeva là is a postposed subject with a relative clause: che + si perdeva (reflexive imperfect ‘was losing itself’ = ‘was disappearing there’).
segments.inferno-25-g126: E introduces the next clause. La sua pelle is the possessor phrase ‘its skin.’ Si facea is a reflexive/medio-passive use of fare in the imperfect meaning ‘was becoming’ or ‘grew,’ so the adjective molle is a predicative complement: ‘its skin was becoming soft.’
segments.inferno-25-g127: E collega la frase seguente: quella di là = ‘that one over there,’ where quella refers back to pelle (feminine) and di là means ‘on the other side / over there.’ Dura is an adjectival predicate ‘is/was hard.’ The copula ‘essere’ is omitted—typical in poetic Italian—so the adjective stands as the result state.
segments.inferno-25-g128: Io vidi = simple past ‘I saw.’ Vidi is followed by the bare infinitive intrar (intrarre, here without final -e by poetic/archaic convention) to form a perception construction: ‘I saw [something] enter.’ Le braccia is the direct object (arms), and per l’ascelle = ‘through the armpits’ uses per = ‘through/into.’
segments.inferno-25-g129: E connects the next noun phrase. I due piè de la fiera = ‘the two feet of the beast’; piè is an archaic/poetic form for piedi/feet, with the numeral due marking quantity and de la fiera marking possession (‘of the beast’).
segments.inferno-25-g130: Ch’eran = che + eran, a relative clause with the archaic imperfect eran (‘they were’). Corti is the predicate adjective ‘short,’ describing the feet; ch’ = che is the relative pronoun ‘which/that.’
segments.inferno-25-g131: Tanto ... quanto is a correlative comparative: tanto ... quanto = ‘as much ... as.’ Allungar is an infinitive ‘to lengthen/stretch out’ used here in parallel with the finite verb accorciavan (imperfect ‘they shortened/drew in’). Quelle is a feminine plural demonstrative ‘those’ (referring back to the arms/braccia), so the clause says the beast’s feet lengthened as much as those shortened.
segments.inferno-25-g132: Poscia is an adverb meaning “afterwards/then.” li is the plural definite article, piè (plural form used poetically) “feet,” and di rietro is the prepositional phrase “from behind/of the rear” indicating these are the hind feet.
segments.inferno-25-g133: insieme is the adverb “together.” attorti is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning “twisted/entwined” and modifies li piè; placed after the noun in the usual Romance adjective position for participial adjectives.
segments.inferno-25-g134: diventaron is the archaic past plural (diventarono) “they became/turned into,” agreeing with the plural subject (the feet). lo membro is the accusative/predicative noun “the limb,” and che l’uom cela is a relative clause “which the man conceals” (cela from celare, present/poetic form meaning “conceals”).
segments.inferno-25-g135: e links the clauses. ’l misero is “the wretched one” (article + adjective/noun). del suo is di + lo = “of his/its own.” n’avea here represents an older clitic construction (n’ stands for ne or a contraction used with the verb) and avea is the imperfect “had.” porti is plural “ports/harbors/openings.” In context the line means the wretch no longer possessed two openings of his own (n’ functions negatively/partitively in older usage).
segments.inferno-25-g136: Mentre che introduces a temporal clause ('while'); ’l is the elided definite article (il) before fummo, so ’l fummo = 'the smoke' (Dante’s spelling fummo = fumo). l’uno e l’altro = 'the one and the other' (articles elided as l’). Vela here functions as a 3rd-person singular present of velare ('veils'); word order is subject (’l fummo) + objects (l’uno e l’altro) + verb (vela) in the poetic line.
segments.inferno-25-g137: e genera is 'and it produces/raises' with genera as 3rd-person singular present of generare; pel = contraction of 'pelo' (hair); suso is an adverb 'upwards/on high'. per l’una parte e da l’altra il dipela = 'on one side and from the other strips it' — il is the direct object pronoun referring back to pel (hair) placed before dipela (verb), dipela is 3rd-person singular present of dipelare 'strip (of hair)'.
segments.inferno-25-g138: l’un si levò e l’altro cadde giuso = 'one rose and the other fell down.' l’un / l’altro are definite (elided) forms; si is the reflexive pronoun used with levò (passato remoto, 3rd singular) so 'rose (himself/itself)'; cadde is passato remoto of cadere 'fell'; giuso is an adverb 'down'. The two clauses are coordinated by e.
segments.inferno-25-g139: non torcendo però le lucerne empie: torcendo is the gerund/participial form of torcere ('twisting/turning') and here is negated by non, so 'not turning.' però means 'however' or 'yet' (contrastive). le lucerne empie = 'the impious/wicked lamps' (lucerne can be literal lamps or figurative eyes); the participle modifies the situation just described (they did not turn their lamps/eyes).
segments.inferno-25-g140: sotto le quai ciascun cambiava muso = 'beneath which each changed his face.' sotto + definite article begins the relative phrase; quai is an archaic/plural relative form (here = 'which' / 'under which') referring back to le lucerne; ciascun is 'each one'; cambiava is the imperfect 'was changing' or 'changed repeatedly' (descriptive/past habitual), and muso means 'expression/face.'
segments.inferno-25-g27: A short locative prepositional phrase: 'Sovra' is the preposition 'over/on/above' placed before the article 'le' and the plural noun 'spalle' (shoulders). It establishes the location for what follows and has no verb.
segments.inferno-25-g28: 'dietro da la coppa' is another locative phrase; 'dietro' = 'behind' and here combines with 'da' + article + noun. 'Coppa' in this context refers to the nape/back part (anatomical 'nape'), so the whole phrase narrows the location to behind the nape.
segments.inferno-25-g29: 'con l’ali aperte' = 'with the wings open/spread', where 'aperte' is a past participle used adjectivally after the noun. The clause then inverts normal order for poetic effect: the unstressed pronoun 'li' (them) refers back to 'le spalle' and precedes the verb 'giacea' (imperfect 'lay'), while the subject 'un draco' follows the verb.
segments.inferno-25-g30: 'e quello affuoca qualunque s’intoppa' uses the vivid historic/present 'affuoca' (literary present here) meaning 'sets on fire' or 'scorches'; 'quello' points back to the dragon; 'qualunque s’intoppa' is a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever/anyone who comes up against (it),' where 's’' is the reflexive clitic and 'intoppa' expresses running into / stumbling upon / coming up against.
segments.inferno-25-g31: 'Lo mio maestro' is an older word order where the article 'Lo' precedes the possessive + noun; it simply means 'my master.' 'disse' is passato remoto (simple past) of 'dire' — 'said.'
segments.inferno-25-g32: 'Questi è Caco' is a demonstrative clause: 'Questi' = 'this one' (subject), 'è' present indicative of 'essere', followed by the proper name 'Caco' (Cacus).
segments.inferno-25-g33: The relative pronoun 'che' introduces a clause that describes Cacus ('who...'). It functions as 'who/that' and links to the following clause.
segments.inferno-25-g34: 'sotto ’l sasso di monte Aventino' is a locative phrase: 'sotto' = 'under', '’l sasso' = 'the rock', and 'di monte Aventino' = 'of Mount Aventine' (genitive). The whole phrase specifies where Cacus acted.
segments.inferno-25-g35: 'di sangue fece spesse volte laco' literally 'made a lake of blood many times': 'fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' (made), 'spesse volte' = 'many times/often' (adverbial phrase), and 'laco' = 'lake/pool' with 'di sangue' indicating material ('of blood').
segments.inferno-25-g36: 'Non va co’ suoi fratei per un cammino' — 'Non va' is present 'does not go'; 'co’' is contracted 'con' (with); 'suoi fratei' = 'his brothers/fellow-retainers' (archaic plural form); 'per un cammino' here means 'along the same way' or 'on the same path.'
segments.inferno-25-g37: 'per lo furto che frodolente fece del grande armento ch’elli ebbe a vicino' = 'because of the theft that he deceitfully committed of the great herd that he had nearby.' 'fece' is passato remoto; 'frodolente' qualifies the theft; 'ch’elli ebbe' = 'which he had' (archaic 'elli' for he).
segments.inferno-25-g38: 'onde cessar le sue opere biece sotto la mazza d’Ercule' — here 'onde' means 'whence/therefore' (result), followed by the infinitive 'cessar' to express resulting cessation: 'hence his wicked works ceased.' 'biece' is an adjective meaning 'wicked' modifying 'opere'; 'sotto la mazza d’Ercule' = 'under Hercules' club' (agent/instrument of cessation).
segments.inferno-25-g39: 'che forse gliene diè cento' — 'che' = 'which'; 'forse' = 'perhaps'; 'gliene' is a clitic combination ('gli' + 'ne') meaning 'to him of it' / 'of them to him' (here 'gave him (of them)'); 'diè' is passato remoto of 'dare' ('gave' — often used of delivering blows).
segments.inferno-25-g40: 'e non sentì le diece' — 'e' = 'and'; 'non sentì' is passato remoto of 'sentire' and here means 'did not feel' (i.e., did not feel ten of them); 'le diece' = 'the ten.'
segments.inferno-25-g41: 'Mentre che sì parlava' = 'while he was speaking thus/so': 'mentre' = 'while', 'sì' = 'thus/so', and 'parlava' is the imperfect tense ('was speaking'), setting simultaneous background action.
segments.inferno-25-g42: 'ed el trascorse' = 'and he passed by'; 'ed' = 'and' (used before a vowel), 'el' is an archaic pronoun 'he', and 'trascorse' is passato remoto meaning 'passed' or 'went by'.
segments.inferno-25-g43: 'e tre spiriti venner sotto noi' — 'e' = 'and'; 'tre spiriti' = 'three spirits'; 'venner' (archaic for vennero) is passato remoto 'came'; 'sotto noi' = 'under/beneath us' (i.e., below our feet).
segments.inferno-25-g44: 'de’ quai né io né ’l duca mio s’accorse' = 'of whom neither I nor my duke/guide noticed': 'de’' = contraction of 'dei'/'de' ('of the'), 'quai' = 'whom/which' (archaic), 'né...né' = 'neither...nor', and 's’accorse' (si accorse) is passato remoto reflexive meaning 'became aware/noticed'.
segments.inferno-25-g45: 'se non quando gridar' is an idiomatic exception: 'se non' = 'except', 'quando' = 'when', followed by the infinitive 'gridar' ('to cry/shout') — together meaning 'except when they cried out'.
segments.inferno-25-g46: Direct speech: 'Chi siete voi?' = 'Who are you?' — 'Chi' interrogative pronoun, 'siete' present indicative 2nd-person plural of 'essere', 'voi' = 'you' (plural).
segments.inferno-25-g82: Li altri due is a poetic word order meaning 'the other two' (li = archaic plural article). ’l is the enclitic object pronoun (archaic clitic form of lo) meaning 'him/it' placed before the verb. Riguardavano is the imperfect indicative, 3rd person plural of riguardare, here meaning 'were looking at' (continuous/past habitual action).
segments.inferno-25-g83: E simply coordinates the clause ('and'). Ciascuno is an indefinite pronoun 'each one' and is treated as singular, so gridava is the imperfect 3rd singular of gridare ('cried out'/'called out'), giving an ongoing/past repeated action.
segments.inferno-25-g84: «Omè is an exclamatory interjection (archaic) expressing pity, dismay, or surprise—roughly 'Alas!' or 'Oh me!'.
segments.inferno-25-g85: Agnel is a vocative form (address) meaning 'lamb' (diminutive/term of endearment or symbolic title); it is used to call or address the person/figure directly.
segments.inferno-25-g86: Come introduces an exclamatory clause meaning 'how' (not 'as'). Ti is the clitic object/reflexive pronoun 'yourself/you' and muti is present indicative 2nd person singular of mutare ('you change'/'you are changing'), giving the force 'how you change!'
segments.inferno-25-g87: Vedi is 2nd person (imperative or present indicative used as an exclamation) of vedere ('see'/'look'), addressing the addressee. Che is the conjunction 'that'. Già is the adverb 'already/by now'. Non is the negation. Se’ is an archaic contracted form of sei ('you are'); the apostrophe shows older spelling. Né ... né is the correlative pair 'neither ... nor', and uno means 'one'.
segments.inferno-25-g88: Già 'already/by then' sets the temporal frame. Eran is the archaic imperfect/pluperfect auxiliary 'were' (3rd plural of essere) used here with the participle to yield 'had become.' Li due capi is the subject 'the two heads' (li = archaic plural article). Un divenuti: un = 'one' and divenuti is the past participle agreeing in number/gender (masc. pl.) meaning 'become'—together 'had become one'.
segments.inferno-25-g89: Quando is the temporal conjunction 'when.' N’ is the clitic form of ne (here 'of it' / 'from it' referring back to the head), attached to the verb. Apparver is the archaic passato remoto (3rd pl.) of apparire ('appeared'). Due figure miste = 'two mixed figures' (figure = shapes/images; miste = past participle used adjectivally 'mixed'). In una faccia is a locative phrase 'on/in one face.'
segments.inferno-25-g90: Ov’ is the contracted form of ove/ov(e) meaning 'where' or 'in which.' Eran is again the past auxiliary 'were.' Due perduti = 'two [that were] lost'; perduti is a past participle agreeing with due and means 'lost/destroyed' here, so the clause means 'where two had been lost.'
segments.inferno-25-g91: Fersi is an archaic past form of farsi (3rd pl.) meaning 'were made' / 'became'—here passive/reflexive in sense 'were made into.' Le braccia is the subject 'the arms.' Di quattro liste indicates composition or division 'out of/four strips.' The verb governs the following description.
segments.inferno-25-g92: A catalog of body parts: le cosce (the thighs) con le gambe (with the legs) e ’l ventre (and the belly) e ’l casso (and the torso/chest). Divenner is the passato remoto 3rd plural of divenire ('became'). Membra is the plural noun 'limbs/parts.' Che is the relative pronoun 'which/that.' Non fuor mai viste is an archaic perfect construction: fuor is an old form related to furono ('they were'), so 'non fuor mai viste' = 'were never seen' ('mai' with negation = 'ever/never'); viste is the past participle 'seen,' agreeing with membra.
segments.inferno-25-g93: Ogne primaio aspetto ivi era casso: — Ogne (indefinite determiner) modifies primaio; primaio is an adjective meaning 'first' or 'primary' placed before the noun as in older Italian. Aspetto is the noun 'appearance/aspect'. Ivi is an adverb 'there'. Era is the imperfect of essere, meaning 'was' (descriptive past state). Casso is an archaic/poetic adjective here meaning 'empty/hollow' (predicate after era). The whole clause states a background condition in the past.
segments.inferno-25-g94: due e nessun l’imagine perversa parea; — The clause uses a paradoxical coordination: due e nessun ('two and none') linked by e used in the 'both...and' sense to express contradiction. L’ imagine is the noun phrase 'the image' (l’ = the). Perversa is an adjective postposed to imagine. Parea is the imperfect of parere 'seemed' and governs the whole noun phrase as its subject (the image seemed...).
segments.inferno-25-g95: e tal sen gio con lento passo. — E is the simple coordinating 'and'. Tal = tale 'such' (qualitative determinative). Sen gio is a two-word adverbial motion: sen (an old form of 's'en'/'si en') + gio (giù) together mean 'downward/away' (movement away/down); here it indicates the direction of motion. Con + noun expresses manner ('with slow step/pace'); lento passo is the manner phrase (lento = slow; passo = step/pace). The clause describes the movement of the image(s).
segments.inferno-25-g96: Come introduces a simile ('as/like'). L' ramarro is 'the lizard' (ramarro = green lizard); note the article contraction l'. Sotto + noun indicates spatial relation 'under'. La gran fersa is an attributive phrase: gran = great/intense; fersa is an older word (here used to evoke a fierce heat or lash of heat in the dog-days) and follows the adjective. Dei dì canicular is a genitive/possessive phrase 'of the dog-days' (dei = of the; dì canicular = dog-day days), specifying the seasonal heat of the simile.
segments.inferno-25-g97: cangiando sepe, — Cangiando is the gerund of cangiare 'changing' (continuous/habitual action in the simile). Sepe ('hedge') is used metaphorically: the phrase means 'changing like a hedge' or 'changing its color as if a hedge', shortening the simile and modifying the prior image (the lizard changing appearance).
segments.inferno-25-g98: folgore par se la via attraversa, — Folgore 'lightning' is the image subject; par is an old/clipped form of parere 'seems'. The clitic se here does not mean 'if' but reinforces the impersonal/seeming construction (in poetry it can be an expletive); la via 'the way' is the object of attraversa, which is a present indicative form 'crosses' (here used impersonally: 'lightning seems to cut across the way'). The order is poetic (subject–verb–object inverted for rhythm).
segments.inferno-25-g99: sì pareva, — Sì 'thus/so' links the simile back to the scene; pareva is imperfect 'seemed', reiterating that what follows was perceived as if by that simile. The comma marks the break between the simile and the narrative return.
segments.inferno-25-g100: venendo verso l’epe de li altri due, — Venendo is the gerund of venire 'coming', indicating simultaneous motion. Verso 'toward' governs the phrase l’epe 'the belly/liver' (epe = belly/liver — here 'belly' of the other two). De li altri due = 'of the other two' (old prepositional contraction; de li = of the). The whole gerundial phrase modifies the participial/main clause that follows.
segments.inferno-25-g101: un serpentello acceso, — Un = indefinite article 'a'. Serpentello is a diminutive noun 'little serpent' (small snake). Acceso is a past participle used adjectivally 'lit/ablaze/aglow' (here 'glowing' or 'fiery'), placed after the noun in typical Italian adjective position.
segments.inferno-25-g102: livido e nero come gran di pepe; — Two coordinate adjectives describe the serpent: livido (pale/bluish/dark — here 'livid/dark in color') and nero 'black'. Come introduces the simile 'like a grain of pepper' (gran di pepe), a condensed comparison emphasizing smallness and blackness.
segments.inferno-25-g103: e quella parte onde prima è preso nostro alimento, — E 'and' links clauses. Quella parte 'that part/area' is the subject of the relative clause. Onde 'from which' introduces a relative clause; prima 'earlier/first' modifies the verb phrase; è preso is a present/passive or perfect-stative construction 'is taken/was taken' (here narrative: 'from which our food was first taken'). Nostro alimento is the object 'our sustenance/food'.
segments.inferno-25-g104: a l’un di lor trafisse; — A + article ('a l’') = 'to' or 'at' the one; l’un di lor = 'one of them'. Trafisse is the passato remoto (or preterite) of trafire 'to pierce', 3rd sing., meaning 'pierced' — it is a simple past action describing the serpent's action toward one of the two.
segments.inferno-25-g105: poi cadde giuso innanzi lui disteso. — Poi 'then' sequences the narrative. Cadde is passato remoto of cadere 'fell'. Giuso = giù, adverb 'down'. Innanzi lui = 'before/in front of him'. Disteso is a past participle used adjectivally 'stretched out' describing the state after falling. The clause reports the subsequent action/result.
segments.inferno-25-g106: Lo trafitto ’l mirò, — Lo trafitto is a nominal phrase 'the struck/wounded one' (trafitto used as a substantive). The following clitic ’l is a typical poetic object clitic standing for 'him/it' (referring elsewhere in the passage). Mirò is the passato remoto of mirare 'to look (at)', so the clause means 'the wounded one looked at him'. The unusual clitic order and elisions are poetic.
segments.inferno-25-g107: ma nulla disse; — Ma = but (contrast). Nulla = 'nothing' (direct object). Disse is passato remoto of dire 'to say'; the clause simply states that he said nothing (negation placed before the verb as is normal: 'said nothing').
segments.inferno-25-g108: anzi, — Anzi is a discourse particle meaning 'rather' or 'on the contrary', used to correct or sharpen the previous clause. It introduces the following corrective detail.
segments.inferno-25-g109: co’ piè fermati, — Co' is the contraction of con + i (with the), here 'with'. Piè is the (poetic) form of piedi 'feet' or 'foot'; fermati is an adjective/participle 'fixed/firm/standing' (literally 'with feet fixed'), so the phrase means 'with his feet planted' indicating he did not move his feet.
segments.inferno-25-g110: sbadigliava pur come sonno o febbre l’assalisse. — Sbadigliava is the imperfect 'he kept yawning' (ongoing habitual or continuous action). Pur (pure) here means 'still' or 'indeed/still'. Come introduces a comparative subordinate clause 'as if' or 'as when': sonno o febbre = 'sleepiness or fever' are the agents of the simile. L’assalisse is the imperfect subjunctive of assalire 'to assail/attack' with the clitic l’ (him) attached; the subjunctive expresses the hypothetical/comparative sense 'as if sleep or fever were attacking him.'
segments.inferno-25-g111: Elli is a stressed subject pronoun “he/it” used to identify the thing/person; l’ serpente is the appositive noun “the serpent,” so the order elli l’ serpente functions as identification (“he was the serpent”). e links the next clause. quei is a demonstrative “that one,” lui is the object pronoun “him,” and riguardava is the imperfect indicative of riguardare meaning “looked at/kept his gaze on.”
segments.inferno-25-g112: l’un ... e l’altro is the correlative pair “the one ... and the other ...” with per indicating source or means (“from/through the wound,” “from the mouth”). fummavan is an imperfect plural (they were emitting/sending out smoke) and forte functions adverbially here (“strongly/densely”). The verbs agree with the two distributed subjects.
segments.inferno-25-g113: e links the action (“and”). ’l fummo is the noun phrase “the smoke” (fummo is an older spelling of fumo) acting as subject. si scontrava is a reflexive/reciprocal construction with scontrare in the imperfect: “the smoke met/collided” (i.e. the plumes struck one another).
segments.inferno-25-g162: Così vid’ io la settima zavorra mutare e trasmutare: an inverted, poetic word order places the adverb Così ('thus') up front for emphasis, followed by a contracted past historic/poetic form vid’ (vidi) + subject pronoun io. La settima zavorra is the feminine singular direct object ('the seventh ballast'), and the two verbs mutare e trasmutare are infinitives coordinated by e and dependent on vid’ io (I saw [it] change and transform).
segments.inferno-25-g163: e qui mi scusi la novità se fior la penna abborra: e 'and' links this clause to the preceding; qui means 'here/at this point' and mi is the indirect object of scusi. Scusi is used in an optative/subjunctive or polite imperative sense ('excuse me') rather than the literal verb sense listed in the lemma. La novità ('the novelty' or newness of the tale) is the thing for which he asks pardon; se fior la penna abborra is a concessive/conditional turn — 'if my pen recoils from the flower' — where fior = 'flower' (metaphorical 'best part') and abborra is a present indicative in a literary register meaning 'shrinks from/abhors'.
segments.inferno-25-g164: E avvegna che li occhi miei confusi fossero alquanto e l’animo smagato: avvegna che is a literary/concessive conjunction meaning 'although' or 'it happens that' and governs the subjunctive; fossero is the imperfect subjunctive (3rd pl) agreeing with li occhi miei (subject) and parallel in sense to l’animo smagato (the latter is a nominative phrase linked by e). Confusi and smagato are predicative adjectives describing the speaker's eyes and spirit respectively; alquanto 'somewhat' moderates the degree.
segments.inferno-25-g165: non poter quei fuggirsi tanto chiusi,: this clause uses non + infinitive (poter) in a past narrative dependent construction ('could not'), with quei referring back to the previously mentioned eyes/people. Fuggirsi is a reflexive infinitive here meaning 'withdraw/escape/turn away' and tanto chiusi is an adverbial phrase 'so (completely) closed' describing how they could not withdraw themselves — a succinct, somewhat archaic syntax common in Dante.
segments.inferno-25-g166: ch’i’ non scorgessi ben Puccio Sciancato;: ch’ is the contracted che = 'that' or 'so that' introducing the subordinate clause; i’ is the tonic article (here functioning like 'I' before the verb) used in older/poetic orthography (actually the object marker 'i' = 'the' is rare here — the phrase reads as ch' i' non scorgessi ben = that I did not clearly discern). Scorgessi is the 1st person imperfect subjunctive (or literary past-subjunctive flavor) 'I did/might discern', used because of the concessive frame; ben 'well' modifies the verb; Puccio Sciancato is the proper name with an epithet (Sciancato = 'the Lame').
segments.inferno-25-g167: ed era quel che sol,: ed 'and' + era 'was' introduces a linking clause identifying someone; quel che sol = 'the one who alone' — sol is an archaic/poetic form of solo ('alone'/'only') placed after che for metrical reasons. The relative che introduces the following restrictive clause.
segments.inferno-25-g168: di tre compagni che venner prima,: di tre compagni is a prepositional phrase 'of three companions' specifying the set; che is the relative pronoun 'who' introducing the subordinate clause; venner is an archaic past tense form (vennero) 'came' (3rd pl. past), and prima 'before/earlier' locates their arrival relative to the speaker's earlier reference.
segments.inferno-25-g169: non era mutato;: a simple past (imperfect) clause with non + era + participle mutato meaning 'had not been changed' or 'was not altered' — mutato is a past participle used predicatively; together they assert that one companion remained unchanged.
segments.inferno-25-g170: l’altr’ era quel che tu,: l’altr’ (l'altro) 'the other' is the subject; era 'was' links to the following relative clause quel che tu — 'the one whom you...' with the clitic/truncated forms kept for meter (apostrophes mark elision). Tu is the 2nd-person pronoun addressed directly in the narrative.
segments.inferno-25-g171: Gaville,: a vocative or appositive proper name placed by itself for emphasis; punctuation and placement mark direct address or specification of whom 'you' refers to.
segments.inferno-25-g172: piagni.: piagni is a present-tense verb form (2nd person singular) from piagnere/piangere in a literary register, meaning 'you bewail/weep for' or 'you lament' — it completes the clause 'the other was the one whom you, Gaville, bewail.' The verb stands alone here to finish the sentence with an emphatic, laconic cadence.
segments.inferno-25-g114: Taccia is a third-person present-subjunctive form used jussively here: 'let ... be silent.' Lucano is the name in the nominative (Lucan) — the implied subject of taccia. Ormai là dov’ e’ tocca is a compact locution: ormai = by now/now; là dov’ = 'there where' (dov’ is an archaic contraction of dove); e’ tocca uses an archaic clitic e’ (egli) with tocca (3rd-person) to mean 'where it touches/concerns.' del misero Sabello e di Nasidio = 'of the wretched Sabellus and of Nasidius,' where del = di + il marks the object/subject-matter touched by 'tocca.'
segments.inferno-25-g115: e attenda a udir is an exhortation continuing the jussive: e = 'and'; attenda is the present subjunctive of attendare used like an imperative 'let him attend/heed' (or 'let him listen'); a + udir = 'to hear.' quel ch’or si scocca = 'that which now si-scocca' — quel = 'that,' ch’ = relative 'that/which,' or = 'now.' si is the reflexive/impersonal marker forming a passive-like construction with scocca (here meaning 'is shot/released'), so 'what is now being released.'
segments.inferno-25-g116: Taccia functions as above ('let ... be silent'); di Cadmo e d’Aretusa = 'concerning Cadmus and Arethusa' (di = about/of); Ovidio is the name in the nominative (Ovid). The whole phrase is an imperative/jussive injunction addressed to the poet Ovid (i.e. 'let Ovid be silent on Cadmus and Arethusa').
segments.inferno-25-g117: ché se introduces an explanatory conditional 'for if ...' (ché = 'for'/'because'; se = 'if'). quello ... quella are demonstrative pronouns pointing back to Cadmus and Arethusa respectively ('that one' / 'that one'); in serpente / in fonte = 'into a serpent' / 'into a spring' with in + noun indicating the result. converte poetando = converte (3rd-person present) 'he turns/transforms' and poetando is a gerund/participial form meaning 'by poetizing' (i.e. through his poetry).
segments.inferno-25-g118: io non lo ’nvidio = 'I do not envy him/it.' io = subject 'I'; non = negation; lo is the direct object clitic 'him/it' (referring to Ovid or his skill); ’nvidio (nvidio) is present indicative 'I envy' with the negation forming 'I do not envy.'
segments.inferno-25-g119: ché = 'for' introducing a reason. due nature = 'two natures' (nature = 'natures'); mai here = 'ever' in a negative sense ('never'); a fronte a fronte is an idiom 'face to face' (fronte = face/front) repeated for emphasis. non trasmutò = trasmutò is passato remoto (he transformed) with non making it negative; sì ch’ = 'so that' (sì = 'so') introducing a result clause; amendue le forme = 'both the forms' (amendue = both) a cambiar lor matera fosser pronte = 'were ready to change their matter' — cambiar = infinitive 'to change,' lor = 'their,' matera = 'matter/material,' fosser is the archaic subjunctive (fossero) 'might be/were,' pronte = 'ready.' Grammatically, the clause says he never so transformed two natures face to face that both forms were ready to change their underlying matter.
segments.inferno-25-g120: Insieme = 'together.' si rispuosero is the passato remoto 3rd-person-plural of rispondere with the reflexive/reciprocal si: here 'they answered/responded (to one another or in agreement).' a tai norme = 'to such rules/norms' (a + tai norme = to such standards).
segments.inferno-25-g121: che introduces a result clause: 'so that ...' ’l serpente = 'the serpent' (l = the); la coda = 'the tail' (la = the); in forca fesse = in + forca ('into a fork' or forked shape) and fesse is the past-subjunctive or archaic past form of fendere/fessare meaning 'split/cleft' (the subjunctive is used in result clauses after che in medieval/poetic usage).
segments.inferno-25-g122: e ’l feruto = 'and the wounded one' (feruto = 'wounded'); ristrinse insieme l’orme = ristrinse (past tense of restringere) = 'drew together/tightened,' insieme = 'together,' l’orme = 'the footprints/tracks.' The clause describes the wounded creature pulling its footsteps close together.
segments.inferno-25-g123: Le gambe con le cosce = 'the legs with the thighs.' seco stesse is an emphatic archaic phrasing: seco = 'with'/'to himself' used here to emphasize union, stesse (feminine plural of stesso) = 'themselves/the same.' s’appiccar = reflexive verb (si + appiccar) 'attached themselves/clung together' with s’ marking the reflexive; sì = 'so' (to indicate degree). Overall: 'The legs, together with the thighs, stuck to one another so.'
segments.inferno-25-g124: che introduces the result: 'so that ...' ’n poco = 'in a little (time)' (che ’n poco = 'so that in a short time'); la giuntura = 'the joint'; non facea segno alcun = 'showed no sign at all' (non + facea, imperfect of fare, + segno + alcun used after negation = 'any'); che si paresse = 'by which it might be seen/recognized' (si paresse is an impersonal/reflexive imperfect subjunctive of parere used with che to mean 'that by which it would seem/appear').
segments.inferno-25-g141: Quel functions as a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' (not the article), ch’ is the enclitic form of che 'who/that', and era is the imperfect indicative of essere 'was' describing a past state. Dritto is a postposed adjective meaning 'upright/standing straight' describing that antecedent.
segments.inferno-25-g142: Il here is a masculine direct object clitic referring back to some part of the face; trasse is passato remoto (3rd sg) of trarre 'drew/pulled'. Ver’ is the poetic contraction of verso 'vers/toward' placed before the article; le tempie is the plural definite object 'the temples'.
segments.inferno-25-g143: E is the simple conjunction 'and'. Di here means 'from' (indicating source) paired with troppa matera 'too much material/flesh'—matera is an older/poetic word for substance or flesh. Ch’ introduces a relative clause; in là venne is a periphrastic way of saying 'came there' (venne = past of venire). Uscir is an infinitive used with venne as a resultive 'came out/emerged'; li orecchi is the article + noun 'the ears' (here the noun sense is not among the provided senses); de le gote = 'from the cheeks' and scempie is an adjective meaning 'maimed/thinned/hollow' describing the cheeks.
segments.inferno-25-g144: Ciò che 'that which' begins a relative clause; non negates corse, here past tense of correre meaning 'ran' or 'moved back'. In dietro = 'backward/behind'. E si ritenne uses the reflexive si with ritenne (past of ritenere) meaning 'kept/retained' or 'remained held'; di quel soverchio = 'of that surplus/excess'.
segments.inferno-25-g145: Fé is the passato remoto of fare used causatively 'made/caused'. Naso a la faccia = 'a nose on the face' (a + article + noun). E le labbra ingrossò is a new clause: ingrossò (passato remoto of ingrossare) 'swelled/enlarged' with le labbra 'the lips' as object. Quanto convenne = 'as much as was fitting'—quanto here = 'as much as' and convenne (passato remoto of convenire) = 'was fitting/appropriate'.
segments.inferno-25-g146: Quel che is again 'the one who/that which'; giacëa is the imperfect of giacere 'lay' describing a continuous past posture. The circumflex/diacritic marks reflect Dante's orthography but do not change the basic imperfect sense.
segments.inferno-25-g147: Il muso = 'the snout/face' (il + muso). Innanzi is an adverb 'forward/in front'; caccia here is the present indicative of cacciare (archaic/poetic spelling) meaning 'drives/thrusts'—so the clause means '(he) thrusts his snout forward'. Note caccia is a verb here despite noun senses given in the list.
segments.inferno-25-g148: E = 'and'. Li orecchi = 'the ears' (li as article + orecchi). Ritira is present indicative of ritirare 'draws back/withdraws'. Per la testa means 'along/by the head'. Come face is an archaic imperfect of fare (face = facea/faceva) 'as (it) used to do' or 'as (it) does'; le corna la lumaccia = 'the horns of the slug/snail' (lumaccia is a slug/snail-like creature).
segments.inferno-25-g149: E la lingua = 'and the tongue' (simple noun phrase; lingua here is the organ/tongue).
segments.inferno-25-g150: Ch’ introduces a relative clause 'which'. Avëa is the imperfect of avere 'had' (note Dante's spelling). Unita is a past participle functioning as an adjective 'joined/together'; e presta = 'and ready' (presta is adjective 'ready/quick'). Prima a parlar = 'before to speak' meaning 'previously ready to speak'—parlar is the infinitive 'to speak'.
segments.inferno-25-g151: Si is the reflexive/clitic indicating the tongue splits of itself; fende is present indicative of fendere 'splits/cleaves'. The verb is intransitive here describing the tongue's action.
segments.inferno-25-g152: E la forcuta = 'and the forked (part)' (forcuta 'forked, bifurcate' used as noun modifier). Ne l’altro = 'in/into the other' (ne here = in/into), si richiude is reflexive present indicative of richiudere 'closes up again' (the forked part closes back into the other part).
segments.inferno-25-g153: E = 'and'. ’L is the elided article 'il' before the noun; fummo in Dante's orthography here means 'fumo' (smoke) — the provided lemma/senses do not match the noun 'smoke', so the appropriate sense is not listed. Resta is present indicative of restare 'remains/stays'.
segments.inferno-25-g154: L’anima: the article L’ (il) marks the feminine noun anima ('soul'); ch’ èra divenuta is a relative clause introduced by the contracted che (ch’) 'that'. Era is the imperfect of essere used here as the auxiliary in the compound past (era + past participle), so era divenuta is the trapassato/trapassato-like form 'had become'. Fiera is used predicatively (noun/adjective) meaning 'ferocious' or 'a wild beast' and divenuta is the past participle 'become', so the clause literally: 'the soul that had become ferocious'.
segments.inferno-25-g155: Suffolando is a present participle/adverbial gerund ('blowing'/'hissing'), giving the manner of the fleeing. Si is the reflexive/clitic particle referring back to the subject (the soul) and marks that the action is performed by the subject itself. Fugge is the present indicative 'flees' (here in narrative present sense), and per la valle is a standard prepositional phrase 'through the valley' indicating direction.
segments.inferno-25-g156: E introduces the coordinated clause 'and ...'. L’altro 'the other' is the subject; dietro a lui is a prepositional phrase 'behind him' (dietro + a + pronoun lui). Parlando is a gerund ('while speaking' / 'speaking') indicating concurrent action, and sputa is the present indicative of sputare 'spits'. The verb is literal and coordinated with the fleeing action in the previous clause.
segments.inferno-25-g157: Poscia 'afterwards/then' is an adverb placing the action in sequence. Li is a clitic pronoun meaning 'to them' (indirect object) placed before the verb; volse is the passato remoto of volgere 'turned' (he turned). Le novelle spalle is a noun phrase 'the fresh/new shoulders' (novelle = 'new'/'fresh', spalle = 'shoulders'), here idiomatically 'he turned his shoulders (i.e. his back) toward them.'
segments.inferno-25-g158: E disse a l’altro: — e 'and' links the act of turning to speech; disse is passato remoto 'said'; a marks the indirect object 'to', and l’altro 'the other' is the recipient. The colon introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-25-g159: I’ vo’ is an archaic contracted speech form: I’ = io 'I', vo’ is here a reduced form of voglio 'I want' (not the literal 'I go'), so together 'I want'. Che introduces a subjunctive subordinate clause. Buoso is the grammatical subject of the subordinate clause, and corra is the present subjunctive of correre 'to run' triggered by the volere ('I want that Buoso run'). Word order places the subject after the complementizer and before the verb.
segments.inferno-25-g160: Com’ (contraction of come) here means 'as' (in the sense 'in the way that'). Ho is the present auxiliary avere 'I have' used with the past participle fatt (fatto) to make the perfect tense; fatt (written truncated as fatt’) is the past participle 'done/made'. Io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I', reiterating who performed the action. Together com’ ho fatt’ io = 'as I have done.'
segments.inferno-25-g161: Carpon is an adverb meaning 'on all fours' or 'crawling' (describing the manner of movement). Per questo calle is a prepositional phrase 'along/through this lane/path' (per = through/along, questo = this, calle = lane/path). The phrase as a whole specifies how and where Buoso should run.

sentences.inferno-25-s5: Throughout all the dark circles of Hell I saw no spirit so proud toward God, not even the one who fell from the walls of Thebes.
sentences.inferno-25-s6: He fled away so that he spoke no more a word; and I saw a centaur, full of rage, come calling: "Where is — where is the harsh one?"
sentences.inferno-25-s8: Maremma! I don't believe the Maremma has as many as he had snakes on his back up to where our lip begins.
sentences.inferno-25-s13: —wherefore our tale stopped, and afterward we still listened to them.
sentences.inferno-25-s14: I did not know them; but he proceeded, as is wont to happen in some case, that one should name another, saying: "Cianfa, where has he ended up?"
sentences.inferno-25-s15: —so that I, in order that the duke might be attentive, placed my finger on from the chin to the nose.
sentences.inferno-25-s16: If you are now, reader, slow to believe what I will say, it will not be a wonder, for I who saw it scarcely consent to it myself.
sentences.inferno-25-s1: At the end of his words the thief raised his hands with both fig‑signs, shouting: "Take them away, God, that I may fling them at you!"
sentences.inferno-25-s3: From then on friendly serpents came upon me, for one of them then wound itself about his neck, as if to say, 'I will not have him speak any more'; another about his arms, and it bound him again, fastening itself so in front that he could not, with them, give a single shake.
sentences.inferno-25-s4: Ah, Pistoia, Pistoia, why do you not resolve to burn yourself up so that you endure no longer, since in doing evil your seed survives?
sentences.inferno-25-s17: As I kept my eyelids raised toward them, a serpent with six feet sprang forward at one of them, and clung to him completely.
sentences.inferno-25-s18: With its middle feet it clasped his belly and with the forefeet seized his arms; then it bit them — one cheek and the other; it stretched its hind parts toward the thighs, placed its tail between the two, and from behind drew it up along the loins.
sentences.inferno-25-s19: Ivy never clung so to trees as the horrible beast, by means of another’s limbs, entwined its own around them.
sentences.inferno-25-s20: Then they stuck together, as if they had been made of hot wax, and mingled their colors; neither the one nor the other any longer seemed what he had been: as, advancing upward from the heat along the papyrus, a brown color runs up that is not yet black and the white dies away.
sentences.inferno-25-s33: The figure that was vanishing there was removing its split tail; its skin grew soft, and the skin over there grew hard.
sentences.inferno-25-s34: I saw arms slip in through the armpits, and the beast’s two feet, which were short, stretch out as much as those (arms) drew in.
sentences.inferno-25-s35: Afterwards the hind feet, twisted together, became the limb that conceals the man, and the wretched creature no longer had two ports of his own.
sentences.inferno-25-s36: While the smoke veiled one and the other with a new color, and raised the hair upward on one side and stripped it from the other, one rose and the other fell down, not, however, turning the impious lamps; beneath which each changed his face.
sentences.inferno-25-s9: On his shoulders, behind the nape, with wings spread a dragon lay upon them; and it sets afire anyone who runs up against it.
sentences.inferno-25-s10: My master said: "This is Cacus, who, beneath the rock of Mount Aventine, many times made a lake of blood."
sentences.inferno-25-s11: He does not go with his brethren along the same road, because of the theft he fraudulently committed of the large herd he had nearby; therefore his wicked deeds ceased under Hercules' club, which perhaps gave him a hundred (blows), and he did not feel the ten.
sentences.inferno-25-s12: While he was speaking thus, he passed by, and three spirits came beneath us; of whom neither I nor my leader took notice, except when they cried "Who are you?"
sentences.inferno-25-s21: The other two were looking at him, and each one cried out: "Alas, little Lamb, how you change!"
sentences.inferno-25-s22: See (or look): you are by now neither two nor one.
sentences.inferno-25-s23: By then the two heads had become one, when from it two mixed figures appeared on one face, where two had been lost.
sentences.inferno-25-s24: The two arms were made of four strips; the thighs with the legs and the belly and the chest became limbs that had never been seen.
sentences.inferno-25-s25: Every first appearance there was empty: the perverse image seemed both two and none; and thus they went down with slow pace.
sentences.inferno-25-s26: As the green lizard beneath the great heat of the dog-days, changing like a hedge, lightning seems to cross the path, so it seemed: coming toward the belly of the other two, a little serpent, aglow, livid and black like a grain of pepper; and that part from which our food was first taken pierced one of them; then it fell down before him, stretched out.
sentences.inferno-25-s27: The one who had been pierced looked at him, but said nothing; rather, with his feet planted he kept yawning, as if sleep or fever were attacking him.
sentences.inferno-25-s28: He was the serpent, and that one kept his gaze on him; one, from the wound, and the other, from the mouth, were sending out strong smoke, and the smoke met.
sentences.inferno-25-s41: Thus I saw the seventh ballast change and be transformed; and here — forgive me the novelty if my pen recoils from the flower.
sentences.inferno-25-s42: And although my eyes were somewhat confused and my spirit dismayed, they could not so withdraw themselves that I did not clearly make out Puccio the Lame; and he was the one who alone, of the three companions who came earlier, had not been changed; the other was the one whom you, Gaville, bewail.
sentences.inferno-25-s29: Let Lucan be silent now where it touches on wretched Sabellus and Nasidius, and attend to hear what is now being released.
sentences.inferno-25-s30: Let Ovid be silent about Cadmus and Arethusa; for if he turns that one into a serpent and that one into a spring by poetizing, I do not envy him; for he never so transformed two natures, face to face, that both forms were ready to change their matter.
sentences.inferno-25-s31: They answered together to such rules, so that the serpent split its tail into a fork, and the wounded one drew his tracks together.
sentences.inferno-25-s32: The legs with the thighs stuck together so closely that in a short time the joint showed no sign by which it might be distinguished.
sentences.inferno-25-s37: The one that had been upright pulled it toward the temples, and from the excess of flesh that went there the ears came out of the hollow cheeks; that which did not run back but was retained from that surplus made a nose on the face and swelled the lips as much as was fitting.
sentences.inferno-25-s38: The one that lay pushed his snout forward, and drew his ears back along his head as the horns of a slug do; and the tongue, which had been joined and ready before to speak, splits, and the forked part closes again into the other; and the smoke remains.
sentences.inferno-25-s39: The soul that had become ferocious flees, hissing, through the valley, and the other behind him spits while speaking.
sentences.inferno-25-s40: Then he turned his fresh shoulders to them, and said to the other: "I want Buoso to run, as I have done, crawling on all fours along this lane."
